questions.question: How many downs does the offense get to move the ball 10 yards?
questions.explanation: Four downs to move 10 yards. If they succeed, they get four new downs. If they fail, the other team gets the ball.

questions.question: How many 15-minute quarters are there in an NFL game?
questions.explanation: Four 15-minute quarters make a 60-minute game (though it takes over 3 hours because of stoppages, timeouts, and commercials).

questions.question: How many points is a touchdown worth?
questions.explanation: A touchdown is worth 6 points. Teams then get to try for 1 extra point (kick) or 2 extra points (another trip to the end zone).

questions.question: What does '3rd and 7' mean?
questions.explanation: It's the third down (try) and the offense needs 7 more yards to get a fresh set of downs. The numbers tell you exactly how many yards they still need.

questions.question: When a team is ahead late in the game and 'runs out the clock,' what are they doing?
options: Calling a timeout to freeze the clock
Running the ball instead of passing to keep the clock moving
Stopping play to check the scoreboard clock
Scoring field goals to run up the score
questions.explanation: Running the ball keeps the clock moving without stops, which wastes the opponent's time and makes it harder for them to score. It's a time-management strategy.

questions.question: What is the main job of the offensive line?
options: Protect the QB and create running paths
questions.explanation: The five offensive linemen protect the quarterback and open running lanes. They're the foundation of every successful offense.

questions.question: Which defensive position is described as 'the chess pieces of the defense'?
questions.explanation: Linebackers can run sideways, diagnose plays instantly, and either rush forward or drop back to cover receivers. They're incredibly versatile.

questions.question: What makes a tight end different from a wide receiver?
options: Tight ends can both block like linemen AND catch like receivers
Tight ends can only run short routes
questions.explanation: Tight ends are hybrids—they can block like linemen to protect the QB or run, and they catch passes like receivers. It's a two-in-one role.

questions.question: In the defensive secondary, what's the main difference between a cornerback and a safety?
options: Safeties line up closer to the line of scrimmage
Cornerbacks cover receivers one-on-one; safeties play deeper and help over the top
questions.explanation: Cornerbacks line up against receivers one-on-one, while safeties play deeper and can help wherever they're needed—they're the last line of defense.

questions.question: Which position is often called 'the household name' because of star recognition?
questions.explanation: The best quarterbacks are household names. Patrick Mahomes, Josh Allen, Jalen Hurts—these are the stars everyone knows. A good QB makes a team competitive.

questions.question: How many points is a field goal worth?
questions.explanation: A field goal (kicking the ball through the uprights) is worth 3 points. It's a key scoring option when the end zone is out of reach.

questions.question: What is the 'red zone'?
options: The area within 20 yards of the opponent's end zone
The area where the defense has an advantage
A penalty zone for excessive celebrations
questions.explanation: The red zone is roughly the last 20 yards before the end zone. Scoring is much easier here because there's less field to work with.

questions.question: After a touchdown, what's the difference between kicking the extra point and going for two?
options: Kicking gets 1 point, going for two gets 2 points if successful
Kicking gets 2 points, going for two gets 3 points
Going for two is only allowed twice per game
questions.explanation: A PAT (extra point kick) from 15 yards away = 1 point. Going for two (advancing the ball from the 2-yard line) = 2 points but riskier.

questions.question: What is a 'safety' in football?
options: When the defense tackles the opponent's ball-carrier in their own end zone
A penalty called on the offense
questions.explanation: A safety (2 points) is rare. It happens when the defense tackles the offense's ball-carrier in their own end zone, which is a huge momentum swing.

questions.question: If an offense is on '4th and goal,' what does that mean?
options: Fourth down and the end zone is close enough to reach
Four quarterbacks are on the field
questions.explanation: Fourth and goal = fourth down with the end zone in reach. Teams usually go for it (try to score) instead of punting because the risk-reward is worth it.

questions.question: What is an 'audible' in football?
options: A loud crowd at the stadium
When the QB changes the play at the line based on what they see
A penalty for talking too much
A replay of the previous play
questions.explanation: An audible is a last-second play change by the QB after reading the defense. It's cinematic and crucial for adjusting to what the defense is showing.

questions.question: What is 'the pocket'?
options: The protected area the offensive line creates around the QB
The area near the end zone
questions.explanation: The pocket is where the QB stands protected by linemen. It usually lasts about 3 seconds before defenders break through.

questions.question: When does the offense NOT want to complete a pass?
options: When about to get sacked and nobody is open; throwing it away avoids a worse outcome
Never—they always want to complete it
questions.explanation: Sometimes an incomplete pass is better than a sack (loss of yards) or interception. A smart QB knows when to chuck it away and reset.

questions.question: What's the difference between a 'pocket passer' and a 'mobile QB'?
options: Pocket passers stay in place; mobile QBs escape and use their legs
questions.explanation: Pocket passers excel at staying in the pocket and throwing with precision. Mobile QBs scramble and beat defenders with their legs. The best can do both.

questions.question: When Mahomes improvises and extends a play, what skill is he using?
options: Scrambling to create time for receivers to get open
questions.explanation: When the pocket breaks down, a great QB like Mahomes scrambles—moves around, buys time, and lets receivers get open in space for a completion.

questions.question: What's the difference between a 3-4 front and a 4-3 front?
options: 3-4 has three linemen/four linebackers; 4-3 has four linemen/three linebackers
3-4 is used only in the playoffs
questions.explanation: Different fronts emphasize different priorities. A 3-4 is flexible and good for pressure. A 4-3 is better at stopping runs and maintaining discipline.

questions.question: What is 'man coverage'?
options: A coverage scheme where everyone covers a zone
One defender is assigned to one receiver all over the field
Only men are allowed to play defense
A penalty called on the offense
questions.explanation: Man coverage = one defender per receiver, following them everywhere. High risk if your corners aren't better than their receivers, but it can shut them down.

questions.question: What does 'Cover 2' mean?
options: Two safeties deep, providing coverage over the top
questions.explanation: Cover 2 = two free safeties playing deep. It prevents long plays but leaves the middle of the field vulnerable to short passes.

questions.question: What is a 'blitz'?
options: When the defense sends more defenders than the offense has blockers
questions.explanation: A blitz forces the QB to get rid of the ball fast. High-risk, high-reward: either you force a sack or the QB finds an open receiver for a big gain.

questions.question: Which defensive team would sack Josh Allen more effectively—a team with elite corners or a team with elite pass-rushers?
options: Elite pass-rushers, who can pressure the QB before he gets his throw off
questions.explanation: Pass-rushers win by getting to the QB fast. Corners win by making him hold the ball longer. Allen can escape, so elite rush is more valuable against him.

questions.question: What is a 'coffin corner kick'?
options: A kick that hits the corner of the goalpost
When a punter lands the ball near the sideline close to the opponent's end zone
questions.explanation: A skill kick where the punter pins the opponent deep in their own territory. It's incredibly valuable because it gives them terrible field position.

questions.question: What is the job of the return specialist?
options: Return to the sideline for instructions
Catch punts or kickoffs and advance the ball downfield
Return the ball to the QB
questions.explanation: Return specialists are explosive players who catch punts or kickoffs and try to get as close to the opponent's end zone as possible. One great return can flip field position.

questions.question: What does a 'fair catch' do?
options: Lets the returner catch the ball without being hit but also without advancing it
questions.explanation: Waving your hand above your head signals a fair catch. You won't be hit, but you can't return it. Sometimes the safe play is the right play.

questions.question: In a close game, why do teams pay so much attention to special teams?
options: One blocked kick, great punt, or long return can shift momentum and field position—worth 4+ points
questions.explanation: In tight games, special teams is where momentum lives. A blocked FG or great return can swing a game. It's the margin of victory.

questions.question: What makes a kicker's job so high-pressure?
options: They work in extreme weather conditions
One miss can lose the game in the final seconds
They have to run the fastest
It's less pressure than other positions
questions.explanation: Kickers often decide games with their leg. A miss on a chip-shot is catastrophic. A 40-yarder under pressure is legendary. The weight is entirely on them.

questions.question: How many regular season games do NFL teams play?
questions.explanation: The NFL regular season is 17 games from September to early January. That's the gauntlet before the playoffs begin.

questions.question: What is a 'division winner' in the NFL?
options: The team that wins a playoff game
The team with the best record in their four-team division
A team that splits wins with all opponents
questions.explanation: Each division has four teams. The team with the most wins in their division automatically makes the playoffs, even with a mediocre record.

questions.question: What is a 'wild card' team?
options: A team with the best record that didn't win their division
A team picked randomly for the playoffs
A team that can pick any opponent
questions.explanation: The three wild card spots go to non-division winners with the best records. They make the playoffs but have the hardest path (play the top seeds first).

questions.question: Why is the #1 seed such a huge advantage?
options: They get to pick their opponents
They get a bye week, play at home all playoffs, and rest while others play
They automatically win the Super Bowl
questions.explanation: The #1 seed gets a week off (bye), then plays every remaining playoff game at home. That's a massive rest and home-field advantage.

questions.question: Can a #7 seed actually win the Super Bowl?
options: Technically yes, but it rarely happens and they'd have to win three straight playoff games
Only if they're the defending champions
No, wild cards are eliminated after one game
questions.explanation: The playoff bracket allows Cinderella stories. A #7 seed can beat everyone and win it all, though it's rare. That's what makes the playoffs beautiful.

questions.question: What's a key thing to notice about the safeties before the snap?
options: Where they're lined up tells you if it's likely a pass or run play
questions.explanation: Deep safeties usually signal a pass play. Safeties closer to the line suggest a run. Reading the safety alignment is the biggest pre-snap key.

questions.question: In the 'red zone,' why does play-calling change?
options: The field is smaller, so there's less room to work and more pressure to score
The offense is required to change plays
questions.explanation: Inside the 20-yard line, the field is compressed. There's less space to work with, so plays get more aggressive and desperation is real.

questions.question: What does it mean when announcers talk about '3rd and long'?
options: The offense needs many yards to get a first down, so a pass is almost guaranteed
questions.explanation: 3rd and long (8+ yards) almost always means a pass play. The offense needs yards, the defense knows they're passing. That's predictable football.

questions.question: During the 'two-minute warning,' what changes?
options: Teams abandon their normal plays for clock management strategy
questions.explanation: With two minutes left in the half or game, every play is about managing the clock. Teams get more conservative or more desperate depending on the score.

questions.question: Which of these tells you a defense is expecting a run play?
options: Linebackers standing up and looking low
questions.explanation: When linebackers are upright and looking low, they're braced for a run. They're expecting the RB to come their way. That's a defensive read.

questions.question: How many points is a free throw worth?
questions.explanation: A free throw (undefended shot from the line) is worth 1 point. Teams get free throws when they're fouled.

questions.question: How long does a team have to shoot the ball in the NBA?
questions.explanation: The 24-second shot clock keeps the game fast. If it hits zero without a shot, the offense loses the ball. That's why basketball never feels slow.

questions.question: How many fouls can an NBA player commit before fouling out?
questions.explanation: Six fouls and you're done for the game. In the NBA's 48-minute format, managing foul trouble is crucial strategy.

questions.question: What is 'traveling' in basketball?
options: Taking more than two steps without dribbling
Passing the ball out of bounds
Moving too fast down the court
questions.explanation: Traveling is taking more than two steps without bouncing the ball. Players get furious when this is called because the rule is nuanced.

questions.question: Why do teams intentionally foul near the end of close games?
options: To reset the clock and give themselves another chance
questions.explanation: Fouling stops the clock and gives the opposing team free throws but resets possession. Late-game fouling is strategic clock management.

questions.question: What is Steph Curry's main contribution to changing how positions work in the NBA?
options: He showed that shooting 25-footers was as valuable as layups
questions.explanation: Curry proved that elite three-point shooting from 35+ feet changes everything. Now centers have to shoot threes, and positions became less defined by size.

questions.question: What is a 'stretch big'?
options: A tall player who can't shoot
A power forward or center who shoots three-pointers
A small player who plays center
A player who stretches before games
questions.explanation: Stretch bigs are big men (4s and 5s) who can shoot from deep. They force defenses to spread out and open up driving lanes.

questions.question: Why is Victor Wembanyama considered position-less?
options: He's 7'4" but can guard everyone and play point-center
questions.explanation: Wembanyama is so versatile—he's tall enough to play center but skilled enough to guard and initiate like a point guard. That's position-less basketball.

questions.question: What is the traditional job of a point guard?
options: Bring the ball up and run the offense
Only defend one type of player
Play the entire game without sitting
questions.explanation: Point guards are the floor generals. They bring the ball up, run plays, and are usually the best ball-handlers and passers on their team.

questions.question: In 2026, why does the 'position name' matter less than it used to?
options: What matters is skill (shooting, passing, IQ) not size or name
All players play multiple positions simultaneously
questions.explanation: Modern basketball cares about what players CAN DO, not their label. A 6'2" forward might guard a 6'4" power forward if they have better feet and discipline.

questions.question: What is the 'pick-and-roll'?
options: One player sets a screen, the ball-handler uses it to get open, then either drives or the screener 'rolls' for a pass
questions.explanation: The pick-and-roll is basketball's bread and butter. It creates confusion and usually results in an open shot or easy bucket. Every team runs it.

questions.question: What is an 'isolation' (iso) in basketball?
options: Teammates clear out; one player goes 1-on-1 against one defender
questions.explanation: An iso is high-risk, high-reward. One player vs. one defender in a cleared-out area. It's main-character energy in concentrated form.

questions.question: What does 'spacing' mean in basketball?
options: The distance between the players and the court edges
Having good shooters spread out to force defenses to extend
How far apart the courts are
questions.explanation: If you have five good shooters on court, defenses can't crowd the paint. That spacing opens driving lanes and creates offense.

questions.question: What is 'transition' basketball?
options: When a team gets a rebound/turnover and is moving toward the other basket fast
questions.explanation: Transition is the fast break—quick passes, easy buckets before the defense sets up. It's why aggressive guards who push pace are so valuable.

questions.question: Why would an elite playmaker like Shai Gilgeous-Alexander prefer making reads mid-possession over running a set play?
options: Mid-possession reads let him react to what the defense is showing, creating better offense
questions.explanation: Great leaders like SGA excel at reading defenses in real-time. If defense adjusts, they counter. That flexibility beats predictable set plays.

questions.question: What is the difference between man-to-man and zone defense?
options: Man = guarding a person; Zone = guarding an area
questions.explanation: Man coverage: you chase your assigned player. Zone: you protect an area. Man is exhausting but flexible. Zone is safer but bad against shooters.

questions.question: What is a 'switch' in basketball defense?
options: Exchanging defensive assignments, usually on a pick-and-roll
questions.explanation: When an offensive pick-and-roll happens, defenders switch assignments. It prevents easy lanes but can create mismatches (like a big guarding a guard).

questions.question: What is 'drop coverage'?
options: Playing defense while dropping the ball
A big defender stays back near the rim instead of coming out to the three-point line
questions.explanation: Drop coverage protects the paint but requires guards to stay tight on the perimeter. If guards get beaten, it fails spectacularly.

questions.question: What is a 'trap' in basketball?
options: Sending two defenders at the ball-handler to force a turnover
questions.explanation: A trap is aggressive defense. Two defenders swarm the ball-handler. High-variance: either you force turnovers or skilled playmakers break it for open shots.

questions.question: Why would a team with elite corners switching on everything have an advantage against pick-and-roll heavy offenses?
options: Elite corners can guard anyone, so they don't get punished when mismatches occur
questions.explanation: If you can switch everything, you eliminate the advantage of creating a big-on-guard mismatch. Elite versatility on defense is championship-level.

questions.question: How many games does each NBA team play in the regular season?
questions.explanation: The NBA regular season is 82 games from October to April. That's a marathon to determine playoff seeding.

questions.question: What is the 'play-in tournament'?
options: A tournament before the regular season
A bracket where seeds 7–10 fight for the final playoff spots
questions.explanation: Added in 2020-21, the play-in tournament makes the stretch run dramatic. Seeds 7-10 play mini-series for the last playoff spots.

questions.question: What is 'tanking' in the NBA?
options: Intentionally losing games late in the season to get a better draft pick
questions.explanation: Controversial but technically allowed: losing on purpose to improve draft odds. It's controversial because it hurts fans and competitive balance.

questions.question: How many games are in each playoff series?
questions.explanation: First team to 4 wins advances. Series can end 4-0, 4-1, 4-2, or 4-3. Each series is a grueling battle.

questions.question: Why does the #1 seed get a bye in the first round of the NBA playoffs?
options: They don't actually get a bye
They earned the best record, so they skip straight to round 2 while seeds 5-8 fight each other
The #2 seed gets the bye instead
questions.explanation: The top two seeds don't play in round one. Seeds 1-2 wait while seeds 3-8 battle. That's a rest advantage for the best teams.

questions.question: What do championship rings symbolize in NBA culture?
questions.explanation: Rings are the ultimate measure of a star's legacy. One ring is good. Three is dynasty. No ring is the gap in every GOAT debate.

questions.question: What is the 'Finals MVP'?
options: The best player on the winning team for the season
The best player in the Finals series
questions.explanation: Finals MVP goes to whoever had the best series—usually the leading scorer but can be a clutch defender. It's the ultimate honor.

questions.question: Why might a star player with tons of individual stats still be considered 'incomplete' without a championship?
options: Rings are the only currency that matters for legacy in basketball culture
questions.explanation: Rightly or wrongly, the NBA culture says: no ring = incomplete career. It's not rational, but it's the culture. Winning is what defines greatness.

questions.question: What is the Larry O'Brien trophy?
questions.explanation: The Larry O'Brien trophy is the hardware. Every Finals-winning team gets it. Along with it come the championship rings for each player.

questions.question: Why is the 2016 Finals (Warriors vs. Cavs) so iconic?
options: The Warriors had a 3-1 lead and lost; LeBron's defense and Kyrie's three won it—legendary game 7
questions.explanation: The Cavs coming back from 3-1 down is one of sports' greatest moments. LeBron's blocks, Kyrie's dagger three—that's Finals lore.

questions.question: What is the 'salary cap'?
options: The maximum amount teams can spend on player salaries per season
The highest a single player can earn
questions.explanation: The salary cap (around $140 million in 2025-26) prevents rich teams from just buying championships. It forces strategic choices.

questions.question: What is an 'unrestricted free agent'?
options: A player who can't be signed
A player with 7+ years in the league who can sign with any team
A player drafted in the first round
questions.explanation: UFAs have player power. They can shop around and sign with whoever offers the best deal. That's when stars move teams.

questions.question: When superstars 'take less money' to team up, what are they betting on?
options: That winning together (and getting rings) is worth the salary cut
questions.explanation: Stars like Durant joining Warriors for less money gamble that winning together gets them legacy and rings. It's a calculated bet.

questions.question: What is the 'luxury tax' in the NBA?
options: A penalty teams pay if they go over the salary cap
questions.explanation: Go over the cap by too much and you pay a luxury tax (penalty dollars). Rich teams in big markets can absorb it; smaller markets can't.

questions.question: Why would the Thunder or Spurs be considered 'amazing' at the draft while other teams struggle?
options: They consistently find talent and develop young players into stars
They're not actually better at drafting
questions.explanation: Some franchises have better scouting, development, and culture. Thunder and Spurs turn draft picks into All-Stars. That's championship building.

questions.question: What is the most important thing to notice about five-on-five matchups?
options: Which player has a favorable matchup and can be attacked
questions.explanation: Games are often decided by one mismatch. A weak defender gets hunted all night. Pros notice who's being targeted and why.

questions.question: What does watching 'off-ball' mean?
options: Watching players without the ball and how they move to create space
questions.explanation: Off-ball movement creates offense. Watch players cut, set screens, relocate for threes. That's where the best basketball happens.

questions.question: Why does 'pace of play' matter?
options: Fast pace favors athletic teams; slow pace favors half-court specialists
All teams play the same speed
questions.explanation: If your team plays slower half-court but the opponent pushes pace, you're getting out-paced. Controlling tempo is a strategy.

questions.question: What is 'rim protection' and why does it matter?
options: A defender's ability to contest shots near the basket
questions.explanation: Great rim protectors (like Victor Wembanyama) make it harder to score in the paint. That's the foundation of good defense.

questions.question: Why would you pick one player to follow for an entire quarter?
options: You'll see how they move, fight for space, and execute—the character development of basketball
questions.explanation: Following one player reveals so much: positioning, effort, off-ball intelligence. That's how you see basketball in HD instead of chaos.

questions.question: What does 'the W' stand for?
questions.explanation: The WNBA—Women's National Basketball Association. 'The W' is the affectionate name used by players, fans, and media. It's the real thing.

questions.question: How many teams are in the WNBA in 2026?
questions.explanation: 15 teams in 2026, including two expansion teams (Toronto Tempo and Portland Fire) that just launched. The league is growing.

questions.question: When does the WNBA regular season run?
questions.explanation: The regular season runs May 16 through September. Teams play 40 games, then the top 8 make the playoffs.

questions.question: Why is 2026 considered a historic WNBA season?
options: Caitlin Clark entered in 2025 and the talent pool is stacked—star power at peak
questions.explanation: Caitlin Clark (generational scorer) plays alongside four-time MVP A'ja Wilson, rising stars, and elite talent. It's the most star-studded season ever.

questions.question: Is the WNBA a 'development league' for the NBA?
options: Yes, it's where players get ready for the NBA
No, it's the top professional home for the best female basketball players in the world
questions.explanation: The WNBA is the destination, not a pipeline. It's the top professional league for elite female players. That's the official position.

questions.question: Is the WNBA 3-point line the same distance as the NBA's?
options: No, the WNBA line is closer—about 1.25 feet closer at the wings
No, the WNBA line is farther
questions.explanation: The closer 3-point line fundamentally changes spacing, offensive strategy, and ball movement. It makes perimeter play and passing more critical.

questions.question: How many minutes are in a WNBA game?
questions.explanation: WNBA games are 40 minutes total. Shorter means less foul trouble, faster pace, and more intensity per possession.

questions.question: Are flagrant fouls called more or less strictly in the WNBA than the NBA?
options: More strictly—loose-ball physicality gets whistled in the W
questions.explanation: The WNBA cracks down on physical play more. Combined with the shorter clock, this rewards efficiency, teamwork, and clean fundamentals.

questions.question: What is different about defensive 3-second violations in the WNBA?
options: The WNBA eliminated the strict defensive 3-in-the-key rule
questions.explanation: Without the strict 3-second violation, centers and forwards can camp the paint longer. That's why rebounding and post-play are more prominent in the W.

questions.question: How would an NBA team's offense change if they suddenly played by WNBA rules?
options: The closer 3-point line and stricter fouls would reward ball movement and shooting over iso-ball
questions.explanation: The rulebook pushes toward possession basketball—more spacing, more movement, less muscle-through-it play. Efficient teams dominate the W.

questions.question: What is the 'playstyle' of the WNBA compared to the NBA?
options: Smaller lineups, more perimeter play, more ball movement, position-less basketball
questions.explanation: The W emphasizes skill, spacing, footwork, and IQ over size. It's guard-friendly, perimeter-friendly basketball.

questions.question: Is position-less basketball more or less common in the WNBA than the NBA?
options: More common—the W values skill over size more aggressively
questions.explanation: The WNBA skews smaller and more skilled. A 6'2" forward might guard a 6'4" power forward because of feet and discipline, not height.

questions.question: Who exemplifies the WNBA's emphasis on skill and spacing?
questions.explanation: Sabrina Ionescu is a 6'2" guard who shoots 35+ foot threes, runs the offense with precision, and exemplifies skill-first basketball.

questions.question: What is a 'stretch forward' or 'stretch center'?
options: A big who can shoot from beyond the 3-point line
A forward who stretches the offense
questions.explanation: Stretch bigs are crucial in the W. They open up spacing and force defenses to adjust. Being a big who can't shoot is increasingly untenable.

questions.question: Why does the W reward 'switch-heavy' defense more than traditional post defense?
options: The spacing and smaller lineups require footwork and IQ, not just size and strength
questions.explanation: With spread-out offenses and emphasis on shooting, switching (staying with whoever ends up in front of you) requires better discipline and footwork than traditional post-up defense.

questions.question: How many MVP awards does A'ja Wilson have?
questions.explanation: A'ja Wilson (Las Vegas Aces) has 4 MVP awards. She's elite on both offense and defense—the model of a two-way star.

questions.question: Why is Caitlin Clark considered a 'generational' player?
options: She's a guard with a three-level scoring game, 40+ foot range, and entered as the #1 pick from Iowa
She's the only player who shoots threes
questions.explanation: Caitlin is a generational scorer with elite range. She's the leading scorer in the league as a 2025 rookie—that's rare air.

questions.question: What makes Angel Reese special as a defender and teammate?
options: She's a dominant rebounder and defender who makes teammates better through infectious energy
questions.explanation: Angel is a 6'3" power forward who is an absolute monster on the glass and defensive. Her energy is infectious.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT mentioned as a top 2026 WNBA star?
questions.explanation: Michael Jordan is a basketball legend but not a 2026 WNBA star. A'ja, Caitlin, and Sabrina are the current stars dominating the league.

questions.question: What is Unrivaled and why is Napheesa Collier significant for starting it?
options: A 3-on-3 off-season league that co-founder Napheesa created to keep stars paid year-round
questions.explanation: Unrivaled is a game-changer—WNBA stars can now play off-season, earn money, and stay game-ready. Napheesa is a co-founder and two-way star.

questions.question: How many games do WNBA teams play in the regular season?
questions.explanation: The WNBA regular season is 40 games (May-early September). Fewer games = every loss matters more. It's compressed and intense.

questions.question: What is the 'Commissioner's Cup'?
options: A mid-season tournament with prize money and fan involvement
questions.explanation: The Commissioner's Cup is a mid-season tournament that gives teams extra incentive and prize money. It's become a big deal.

questions.question: How many teams make the WNBA playoffs?
questions.explanation: Top 8 teams make it. No conference split—it's a pure standings race. Being 8th is brutal because you start in the hardest playoff spot.

questions.question: Is there a playoff bye in the WNBA like there is in the NBA?
options: Yes, the #1 seed gets a bye
Yes, seeds 1 and 2 get a bye
No, all 8 teams play in round one
questions.explanation: The #1 seed gets a bye and plays in the second round while seeds 3-8 battle. It's a huge advantage.

questions.question: What format is the WNBA Finals?
questions.explanation: The Finals are best-of-5, played in late September or early October. First team to 3 wins is champion. It's electric and decisive.

questions.question: Which college programs are traditional powerhouses that send players to the WNBA?
questions.explanation: Iowa, UConn, and Stanford consistently send multiple players to the league. Caitlin Clark came from Iowa. Paige from UConn. That's your pipeline.

questions.question: When is the WNBA Draft held each year?
questions.explanation: The draft is in April, a few weeks before the season starts. Top college players are scouted obsessively. It's where the league's future comes from.

questions.question: What is Unrivaled and when does it play?
options: A college league, plays during school year
A 3-on-3 off-season league (November-March) founded by WNBA stars
questions.explanation: Unrivaled is where WNBA stars play off-season. It's smaller courts, 3-on-3, faster games. It keeps players paid and game-ready.

questions.question: Why would a WNBA star choose Unrivaled over playing internationally in the off-season?
options: They can stay home, build brand, earn money, and play elite hoops
questions.explanation: Historically, WNBA stars played overseas off-season. Unrivaled changes that calculus—they stay home, stay in the US market, earn good money.

questions.question: What is the basketball calendar now for elite women's players?
questions.explanation: It's year-round now: WNBA regular season (May-Sept), Finals (Sept-Oct), Unrivaled (Nov-March), Draft prep (April). The W is a career.

questions.question: According to the Euphoria framing, who is the 'Cassie' of the offense?
options: The quarterback—the face of the show, camera-loved, critiqued every Monday
questions.explanation: The QB is the Cassie—the protagonist who drives every play. When the team wins, they're a genius. When they lose, they're a tragedy.

questions.question: What role do the offensive linemen play in the Euphoria metaphor?
options: They're the Faye—five enormous people whose job is protection, loyal, undersold, noticed when something goes wrong
questions.explanation: The O-line is the Faye—pillars of the production. Invisible when working, blamed when they fail. That's loyalty and undersale in one.

questions.question: How long does the QB have to act on the snap before defenders reach him?
questions.explanation: Three seconds. That's the pocket window. Receivers run routes, the QB reads, and chaos erupts. It's all contained in three seconds of drama.

questions.question: How many total plays happen per game, roughly?
questions.explanation: 60-70 plays per game. Each is a three-second short film. Multiply that by timeouts, commercials, and slow-mo replays and you get a three-hour episode.

questions.question: In the Euphoria framing, what does the 'red zone' represent?
options: The danger zone where air gets thin
The area where cameras zoom in and defenses tighten—the show's most-watched scenes
The area where the most penalties occur
questions.explanation: The red zone is the story's most-climactic area. Every play matters. The camera finds it. The drama is maximum. That's where the art happens.

questions.question: According to the Euphoria framing, who is the 'Maddy' of basketball?
options: The point guard—the protagonist who brings the ball up, calls the play, makes decisions, is fast and sharp
questions.explanation: The PG is the Maddy—the protagonist. Steph Curry, SGA, Jalen Brunson—these are main-character energy in concentrated form.

questions.question: What makes basketball different from football in the Euphoria framing?
options: Basketball is a continuous take with no huddles; football is cut to cut
questions.explanation: Football cuts between plays. Basketball is one long dance scene—continuous, no breaks, everything happening at once. That's the core difference.

questions.question: How much time does the offense have to shoot in the NBA shot clock?
questions.explanation: 24 seconds. That's why basketball never feels slow—the clock keeps moving, the pace is relentless, it's all momentum and flow.

questions.question: What is the 'step-back three' in Euphoria terms?
options: Maximum drama—the camera-ready shot where the shooter creates space and launches from distance
questions.explanation: The step-back three is the Euphoria moment—slow-mo, high drama, main-character energy. Everyone knows what it means when it goes in.

questions.question: Why is off-ball movement the most important thing to watch according to the lesson?
options: The best basketball happens off the ball—cuts, screens, movement—not in the possession itself
The ball is always the focus
questions.explanation: Watch the four players without the ball. That's where the dance happens. The shot at the end is just punctuation—the sentence was written off-ball.
